Name: CurvatureAwareRoutingForMixedPrecisionTraining
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing for Mixed-Precision Training in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: A curvature-aware precision routing framework can dynamically adjust numerical precision during training to optimize both computational efficiency and numerical stability, leading to faster convergence and improved model accuracy.
Related Work: Previous work in mixed-precision training has primarily focused on static precision switching or heuristic-based methods. For instance, Mix-GEMM (Reggiani et al., 2023) and Tri-Accel (Sheibanian et al., 2025) propose hardware-software co-designed solutions and adaptive optimization strategies, respectively. However, these approaches lack a formalized curvature-aware mechanism for precision control. Our proposal introduces a curvature-aware precision routing protocol that leverages spectral quantization theory and geometric subspace diagnostics to dynamically adjust precision, ensuring both numerical stability and computational efficiency.
Abstract: We propose a curvature-aware precision routing framework for mixed-precision training in neural networks. This framework dynamically adjusts the numerical precision of computations based on curvature information derived from the Hessian matrix and gradient covariance. By leveraging spectral quantization theory and geometric subspace diagnostics, our method ensures numerical stability and computational efficiency. We validate our approach through empirical evaluations on standard benchmarks, demonstrating faster convergence and improved model accuracy compared to static mixed-precision training and heuristic-based methods.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on a variety of neural network architectures (e.g., ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B0) and datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100).

Compare the performance of our curvature-aware precision routing framework against static mixed-precision training (FP16/FP32) and heuristic-based precision control methods in terms of training time, memory usage, and model accuracy.
Conduct ablation studies to assess the impact of different components of the framework, such as spectral quantization, Hessian conditioning, and gradient covariance decay.
Perform systematic evaluations using PAC-Bayes generalization bounds to quantify the theoretical guarantees provided by the curvature-aware precision routing framework.
Analyze the robustness of the framework under different initializations and learning rates to ensure its reliability and generalizability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of curvature diagnostics and spectral quantization may introduce additional complexity, which could be mitigated by optimizing the implementation and using efficient numerical libraries.
The framework's performance may vary across different network architectures and datasets, requiring careful tuning and validation.
The theoretical guarantees provided by PAC-Bayes bounds may not always translate to practical improvements in model accuracy, necessitating empirical validation.
